metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the blast-radius assertion conflict: remove the unguarded `census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False` form from the Signal definitions bullet and canonicalize on the Round-6 instruction (`check_blast_radius(...)` returning `BreakerVerdict.allowed == False`, or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)` with explicit kwargs).
findings.finding: The Signal definitions section and the Round-6 corrections directly contradict each other on how to assert the blast-radius breaker: Signal defs say `assert via... census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False` (unguarded index), while Round-6 says 'do NOT index ["breaker"] unguarded' and instead use `check_blast_radius(...)` returning `BreakerVerdict` or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)`. An executing agent cannot choose between two mutually incompatible code paths.
findings.location: Signal definitions > Blast-radius breaker bullet AND Round-6 corrections > BREAKER ASSERTION bullet
findings.suggested_fix: In the Signal definitions blast-radius bullet, replace `assert via the breaker API in classify.py: census()['breaker']['allowed'] == False (BreakerVerdict.allowed; both live in classify.py...)` with: `assert via check_blast_radius(acting=N, total=M, max_fraction=0.09) returning BreakerVerdict(allowed=False) (classify.py), or equivalently census(max_acting_fraction=0.09, total_bindings=M)['breaker']['allowed'] == False; do NOT call bare census()['breaker']`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fallback bounding assertion for C1 that the executor can implement before C1 lands — e.g., 'assert the conflict record `feline-wandering-dassierat` exists with a non-null `conflict_type` field and the `rebar-id:<local_id>` label; update to C1's exact field once C1 is manifest' — so the harness is writable in advance.
findings.finding: The C1 signal explicitly defers the assertion design to execution time: 'reference C1's implemented signal at validation time (C1 is pre-manifest; do NOT hard-code an unverified field)'. An executor cannot write the C1 assertion in advance and must 'figure out' the correct field/value at runtime — this is a placeholder deferring a real design decision.
findings.location: Signal definitions > C1 bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the C4 jitter proving mechanism: e.g., 'seed the RNG / mock `random.uniform` to return a fixed value; assert the observed sleep duration equals base_delay + fixed_jitter within ±5 ms; or inspect the retry log for a sleep value in [base, base+max_jitter]' — so the jitter assertion is deterministic.
findings.finding: C4's proving criterion 'assert the JITTERED-BACKOFF behavior (a simulated 429 exit -> backoff -> success)' specifies no mechanism for asserting jitter — jitter is non-deterministic by design. There is no stated approach (e.g., mock time source, timing-window bound, log-line inspection, or call-count check) to make this assertion deterministic and CI-stable.
findings.location: Signal definitions > C4 bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the default report path in the plan (e.g., `test-results/rebar-e2e-report.xml`, or `$REPORT_PATH` defaulting to that value) so the harness and CI attachment step agree without executor guesswork.
findings.finding: AC2 requires the harness to write 'a JSON/JUnit report to a named path' but the path is never named anywhere in the plan. An executor must choose an arbitrary path, making the CI artifact attachment step non-reproducible across runs and teams.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria > AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2: 'writes a JUnit XML report to `test-results/rebar-e2e-report.xml` (override via `REBAR_E2E_REPORT_PATH` env var)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete proving command for the isolation criterion, e.g., 'assert `git diff origin/main -- <prod_env_path>` is empty post-run, and Jira audit log for the production project shows zero writes from the service account during the test window'.
findings.finding: AC5 states 'production REB + tickets branch untouched' as a completion criterion but provides no proving command or observable artifact to verify this negative assertion. There is no specified diff check, branch-state assertion, or audit log query that would confirm production was unaffected.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria > AC5

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reframe the ADR criterion as an observable end-state with a location, e.g., 'a merged `docs/adr/NNN-e2e-harness-pattern.md` present on the default branch describing the bilateral teardown pattern' so it is repo-checkable.
findings.finding: AC6 includes 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note' — this is effort-framed (describes an activity, not an observable outcome) and has no stated observable end-state or in-session evaluable artifact (e.g., a file path, a PR containing the ADR, a linked doc URL).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria > AC6
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note' with 'a file `docs/adr/NNN-e2e-harness-pattern.md` merged to main, covering the bilateral teardown pattern and re-run procedure'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the Acceptance Criteria (and Signal definitions) blast-radius assertion to match the Round-6 correction: use `check_blast_radius(...).allowed == False` or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)['breaker']['allowed'] == False` with explicit kwargs — remove the bare unguarded `census()['breaker']` form from both sections.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria line for blast-radius still specifies the bare unguarded form `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False`, which Round-6 corrections explicitly warn will KeyError on a non-acting pass. The AC was not updated to reflect the Round-6 correction, creating a contradiction between sections.
findings.location: AC line: 'blast-radius via `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False`' vs Round-6 corrections section: 'do NOT index `["breaker"]` unguarded'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace in AC and Signal definitions: `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` → `classify.check_blast_radius(acting=N, total=M).allowed == False` (or call `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)['breaker']['allowed']` with explicit kwargs as Round-6 specifies).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile AC line 3 and the Harness section with the Round-6 teardown-retry correction: either update AC to say 'reuse an existing app-layer backoff helper for teardown retry' or explicitly justify why a bespoke loop is preferred here, so both sections agree.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria still specifies a 'bespoke 3x exp-backoff' loop for teardown retry, while Round-6 corrections say to 'reuse an existing backoff helper (e.g. the app-layer retry in `dispatch_one`/`batch_dispatch`) rather than a bespoke 3x loop, where practical.' The AC was not reconciled with the Round-6 correction.
findings.location: AC line 3: 'Teardown-failure fallback (3x exp-backoff, `leaked-artifacts.log` CI artifact, non-zero exit)' vs Round-6 corrections: 'TEARDOWN RETRY: reuse an existing backoff helper... rather than a bespoke 3x loop, where practical'
findings.suggested_fix: Update AC line 3 to: 'Teardown-failure fallback (3x exp-backoff via existing retry helper, `leaked-artifacts.log` CI artifact, non-zero exit)' and add a note in the Harness section that the existing `dispatch_one`/`batch_dispatch` retry utility should be reused.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief description in the plan body of what the ADR/design note must cover (harness pattern, teardown guarantee, isolation approach, re-runnability) and where it lives (repo path or ticket attachment) so the AC criterion is verifiable.
findings.finding: The ADR/design note is listed as a required deliverable in AC ('harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note') but the plan body contains no description of what the ADR should cover, its location, or who owns it — leaving the criterion unanchored to any described work.
findings.location: AC line 5: 'harness pattern recorded in an ADR/design note'; no corresponding section in plan body.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Promote N (oscillation pass count) and the teardown retry limit to named constants (e.g. `OSCILLATION_PASSES`, `TEARDOWN_MAX_RETRIES`) defined in one place in the harness, so they can be adjusted without grep-and-replace across the spec/code.
findings.finding: Harness-internal thresholds N=3 (oscillation passes) and 3x (teardown retries) are hardcoded literals in the spec with no indication they will be named constants or configurable; these are tunable values likely to evolve as the reconciler's pass frequency or CI environment changes.
findings.location: AC line: 'N (oscillation passes) = 3'; AC line: 'Teardown-failure fallback (3x exp-backoff...)'; Harness section: '3x with exponential backoff'
findings.suggested_fix: In the harness entry-point (or a `conftest.py` / top-level bash variable block), define `OSCILLATION_PASSES=3` and `TEARDOWN_MAX_RETRIES=3` as named constants and reference them throughout; the AC can read 'N=OSCILLATION_PASSES (default 3)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that synthetic artifact IDs are appended to a durable temp file (e.g., `created-artifacts.tmp`) atomically as each artifact is created, so the teardown step can read and clean them even after a process kill — not solely from in-memory state.
findings.finding: The partial-setup artifact tracking that enables guaranteed bilateral teardown is not specified to be written durably as each artifact is created. The plan says the harness 'tracks WHICH of N synthetic artifacts were created (partial-setup)' but does not state that IDs are persisted to disk immediately upon creation (write-ahead style). If the harness process is killed (SIGKILL, OOM, CI runner timeout) between artifact creation and the yield/finally block, an in-memory tracking list is lost and teardown cannot clean those artifacts — the 'guaranteed' property silently fails precisely when durability matters most.
findings.location: Harness section: 'tracks WHICH of N synthetic artifacts were created (partial-setup) so teardown removes exactly those'
findings.suggested_fix: In the harness, open a per-run temp file (e.g., `{run_id}-created-artifacts.tmp`) before setup begins; after each successful `create_issue` call, `fsync`-append the returned ID. The teardown step reads this file (if it exists) and deletes all listed IDs, then removes the temp file. This file becomes the authoritative partial-setup record regardless of how the process terminates.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a per-call timeout (e.g., 30 s) to all harness-side Jira API calls (setup probe, create/delete in teardown, matrix step calls), distinct from the C4 retry-backoff under test, so a hung Jira call fails fast with a clear timeout error rather than blocking CI indefinitely.
findings.finding: The harness makes live Jira API calls (create issue, delete issue, field/status mutations) throughout the validation run, but no per-call timeout is specified for these calls in the harness itself. The C4 signal tests the production retry path, but the harness's own Jira calls (setup probe, teardown, non-C4 matrix steps) have no timeout budget. A Jira hang on any of these calls would cause the entire CI job to block until the CI runner's global wall-clock timeout, with no intermediate error surfaced.
findings.location: Harness section / Setup probe / Acceptance Criteria — no timeout specified for harness-side Jira calls

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that `leaked-artifacts.log` is either (a) scoped to the per-run CI workspace (e.g., `$WORKSPACE/leaked-artifacts.log`) so parallel jobs write separate files that are aggregated by the CI system, or (b) written with a run-unique prefix per line (run ID + artifact ID) and uses an append-only atomic write to a shared path.
findings.finding: The plan specifies appending leaked artifact IDs to `leaked-artifacts.log` on teardown exhaustion but does not specify whether this file is scoped per CI workspace/run or is a shared path. If it is a shared path and multiple CI jobs run in parallel (e.g., on a shared agent or in a matrix build), concurrent appends from different jobs could interleave partial lines, corrupting the log and making it unusable for artifact recovery.
findings.location: Harness section: 'append the id to leaked-artifacts.log (a CI artifact)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact JSON/JUnit report output path and filename pattern (e.g. `test-results/rebar-e2e-report.xml`) in the AC or Harness section, and document the CI artifact upload step so the reporting criterion is end-state-observable.
findings.finding: The JSON/JUnit report 'named path' is referenced in the AC but never actually named or specified anywhere in the plan, leaving the observable proof for the reporting criterion incomplete — an implementer won't know where to write the report or what the CI artifact path should be.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'writes a JSON/JUnit report to a named path attached to the ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Harness section: 'Report written to `test-results/rebar-live-validation.json` (JUnit mirror at `test-results/rebar-live-validation.xml`); CI uploads both as artifacts named `rebar-e2e-report`.' Update the AC line accordingly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` in AC line 2 with `classify.check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings, max_acting_fraction).allowed == False` (or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)['breaker']['allowed']`), matching the Round-6 correction that the AC body failed to propagate.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria line 2 still specifies `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` unguarded, which the Round-6 corrections already note will KeyError on a non-acting pass (census() without both kwargs). The codebase has the direct, purpose-built function `classify.check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings, max_acting_fraction)` (classify.py:305, called at classify.py:369 and binding_walk.py:238) that returns a `BreakerVerdict` with `.allowed`. The AC instructs implementors to call `census()` — a higher-order telemetry aggregator — instead of the direct breaker API, despite Round-6 explicitly correcting this. This is an abstraction-misuse: the wrong sub-function of the classify module is canonicalized in the AC when the direct purpose-built function already exists.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 2: `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False`
findings.suggested_fix: AC line 2: replace `assert via... census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` with `assert check_blast_radius(acting_decisions, total_bindings=N, max_acting_fraction=0.09).allowed == False` — this directly calls the purpose-built function (classify.py:305) that `binding_walk.py` already uses in production.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the teardown-failure fallback AC to explicitly mandate reuse of `_call_with_retry` from `dispatch_one`/`batch_dispatch` (or name the specific import path) rather than permitting a bespoke loop.
findings.finding: The teardown-failure fallback is specified as 'retries 3x with exponential backoff' (a bespoke loop), while the codebase already has `_call_with_retry` in dispatch_one.py (16+ call-sites across apply_outbound.py, apply_inbound.py, apply_inbound_records.py, batch_dispatch.py, applier.py, dispatch_one.py) that implements exactly this contract (exponential backoff, configurable max_retries, 429/5xx handling). Round-6 corrections already instruct 'reuse an existing backoff helper...rather than a bespoke 3x loop, where practical,' but the AC wording ('retries 3x with exponential backoff') doesn't enforce this — leaving an implementor free to build a NIH duplicate. The 'where practical' qualifier is vague enough to be ignored.
findings.location: Harness section: 'each cleanup call retries 3x with exponential backoff'; AC line 3; Round-6 corrections
findings.suggested_fix: AC line 3: 'Teardown-failure fallback: each cleanup call wrapped in `_call_with_retry(client.delete_issue, key, max_retries=3)` (from `dispatch_one`); on `RetryExhaustedError` append id to `leaked-artifacts.log` and exit non-zero.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the line reference in the Harness section: `acli.py:721-723` → `acli.py:715-717` (that is where `raise PermissionError(msg)` actually lives in `delete_issue()`).
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'acli.py:721-723 RAISES PermissionError on missing delete perm' but the PermissionError raise is at lines 715–717 and delete_issue() ends at line 719; lines 721–723 don't exist in the function. This stale line reference could cause a harness author to look at the wrong location when instrumenting the probe or debugging a scope misconfig.
findings.location: Harness section: 'NOTE acli.py:721-723 RAISES PermissionError on missing delete perm'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'acli.py:721-723' with 'acli.py:715-717' in the setup-probe note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the C4 signal definition to note that `_call_with_backoff` no longer exists in the codebase (it was removed entirely, not just left as dead code), so the exclusion guidance is accurate: the ONLY live path to patch is `_run_acli` in `acli_subprocess.py`.
findings.finding: The plan refers to 'the dead `_call_with_backoff`' as if it exists as dead code in the codebase, but a full codebase grep finds zero occurrences of `def _call_with_backoff` anywhere — it has been fully removed, not merely left as unreachable dead code. The plan's C4 section says 'NOT the dead `_call_with_backoff`', which implies the harness should avoid it; if an implementer searches for it to confirm the exclusion they will find nothing.
findings.location: Signal definitions: C4 section — 'NOT the dead `_call_with_backoff`'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'NOT the dead `_call_with_backoff`' to 'NOTE: `_call_with_backoff` no longer exists in the codebase (fully removed); the sole live 429-retry path is `_run_acli` in `acli_subprocess.py`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the AC blast-radius line to use the Round-6-corrected form: either `check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings, max_acting_fraction).allowed == False` or `census(decisions, max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...) ['breaker']['allowed'] == False` — do NOT leave the bare unguarded `census()['breaker']` in the AC.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria section still contains the unguarded form `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` without the required `max_acting_fraction` and `total_bindings` kwargs. The Round-6 corrections in the narrative body correctly state that a bare `census()['breaker']` KeyErrors, but the AC line was not updated to match — creating a contradiction between the AC and the Round-6 guidance. A harness author following the AC literally will get a KeyError.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, second bullet: '...blast-radius via `classify.census()["breaker"]["allowed"]==False`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` in the AC with `classify.check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings, max_acting_fraction).allowed == False` (per Round-6 correction guidance already in the plan body).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the Setup probe line reference from `acli.py:721-723` to `acli.py:717` (the actual `raise PermissionError` for 403 responses in `delete_issue`).
findings.finding: The plan cites `acli.py:721-723` as the location of the PermissionError raise in the Setup probe section, but the actual raise is at line 717 (`raise PermissionError(msg) from exc`). Lines 721-723 do not exist or contain unrelated code. This is a stale/off line reference.
findings.location: Harness section, Setup probe: 'NOTE `acli.py:721-723` RAISES PermissionError on missing delete perm'
findings.suggested_fix: Change `acli.py:721-723` to `acli.py:717` in the Setup probe note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Update the AC blast-radius assertion from the bare `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` to `check_blast_radius(...).allowed == False` (or `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)['breaker']['allowed']`) to match Round-6 corrections.
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria line for blast-radius still specifies `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` — the bare unguarded form — even though Round-6 corrections in the same plan explicitly warn this KeyErrors on a non-acting pass and instruct the implementer to use `check_blast_radius(...)` returning a `BreakerVerdict` or call `census(max_acting_fraction=..., total_bindings=...)` explicitly. The AC was not updated to match Round-6.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, bullet 2: 'blast-radius via `classify.census()["breaker"]["allowed"]==False`'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` in AC bullet 2 with `classify.check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings=N, max_acting_fraction=0.09).allowed == False` to make the AC consistent with Round-6.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Round-6 TEARDOWN RETRY that the recommended pattern is `_call_with_retry(client.delete_issue, jira_key, max_retries=3)` (confirming `delete_issue` is callable-wrapped-compatible), or name an alternative if it is not.
findings.finding: The Round-6 correction recommends reusing `dispatch_one._call_with_retry` (or `batch_dispatch._call_with_retry`) as the teardown backoff helper, but that helper's interface (`fn, *args, timeout_s, max_retries, **kwargs`) is designed to wrap Jira API callable objects — not CLI-subprocess delete invocations. The teardown path calls `client.delete_issue(jira_key)` which does route through `_run_acli`, so `_call_with_retry(client.delete_issue, jira_key)` would technically work, but the plan does not confirm this connection, leaving an ambiguity that could cause the implementer to write a bespoke loop instead.
findings.location: Round-6 corrections, TEARDOWN RETRY: 'reuse an existing backoff helper (e.g. the app-layer retry in `dispatch_one`/`batch_dispatch`)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the echo-suppression signal covers TWO distinct mechanisms: (1) `mutation_count==0` on the second pass is achieved by pre-seeding the baseline via `binding_store.seed_baselines_from_snapshot()` (or running a full first pass), NOT via `ProvenanceLedger`; (2) `ProvenanceLedger`-driven suppression applies only to collection-field elements (labels/watchers/links) via `compute_mutations_with_ledger()` — update the signal definition to name both mechanisms separately and specify which harness action exercises which.
findings.finding: The echo-suppression signal description conflates two distinct codebase mechanisms: `ProvenanceLedger` (conflict_resolver.py:227) — which handles element-level echo suppression for *collection* fields (labels, watchers, links) via `compute_mutations_with_ledger()` — and `binding_store.set_baseline()` / `seed_baselines_from_snapshot()` — which seeds the per-pair field/status baseline that drives the three-way merge and produces `mutation_count`. The plan says 'field/status writes are baseline-seeded via the ProvenanceLedger' but the ProvenanceLedger has no baseline-seeding role for scalar fields; it carries `_records` keyed by element (e.g. `'labels:rebar-status:blocked'`). An implementer who follows the plan literally will seed the wrong subsystem and the `mutation_count==0` assertion will either always pass vacuously (if the ledger is never consulted on a second pass for scalars) or fail for the wrong reason, masking real echo bugs.
findings.location: Signal definitions > Echo suppression
findings.suggested_fix: Split the echo suppression signal into two explicit sub-assertions: (a) scalar-field echo: harness runs pass 1 (establishes baseline via `seed_baselines_from_snapshot`), then pass 2 over the same data asserts `reconcile_once(...)["mutation_count"] == 0`; (b) collection-element echo: after a label write, the `ProvenanceLedger` contains the element key and `compute_mutations_with_ledger` suppresses the re-emit on pass 2. Name the seeding mechanism explicitly as `binding_store.seed_baselines_from_snapshot()` or 'a real prior pass'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive selection criterion for the harness approach: state that the pytest autouse fixture is the preferred path because AC1 requires programmatic failure injection (RuntimeError after create) that a bash trap cannot express cleanly; the existing e2e_validation_probe.sh may be referenced for Jira interaction patterns but is not the harness foundation.
findings.finding: The harness approach selection ("Build on the existing e2e_validation_probe.sh if suitable; else a pytest autouse fixture yield+finally (or bash trap cleanup EXIT)") offers no stated criterion for which branch to take, giving the implementer no positive rationale to evaluate suitability. The existing probe (scripts/jira-pressure-test/README.md) is explicitly documented as manual-only and not CI-wired, yet the plan treats it as potentially usable without identifying what makes it 'suitable.' The absence of a decision criterion means the implementer may default to the bash probe path, which lacks the test-injection capability needed for AC1 (teardown-under-failure test that injects a RuntimeError after create).
findings.location: Harness (guaranteed bilateral teardown) section; AC line 'Harness with GUARANTEED bilateral teardown'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'Build on the existing e2e_validation_probe.sh if suitable; else a pytest autouse fixture' with: 'Use a pytest autouse fixture (yield + finally) as the primary harness — it is the only approach that can satisfy AC1's failure-injection requirement (RuntimeError after create). Jira interaction patterns from e2e_validation_probe.sh may be adapted as helper functions within the pytest suite.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-paragraph justification for why the existing `e2e_validation_probe.sh` / `e2e_field_validation_probe.sh` are not extended (e.g., they lack `trap cleanup EXIT`, partial-setup tracking, and `leaked-artifacts.log`), and state whether the new harness supersedes or coexists with them.
findings.finding: The plan proposes building a new bilateral-teardown harness (pytest autouse fixture or bash `trap cleanup EXIT`) without explaining why the existing `e2e_validation_probe.sh` and `e2e_field_validation_probe.sh` — both of which already exercise live Jira and do bilateral cleanup in a Phase 8 block — are not extended instead. The existing probes lack `trap cleanup EXIT` (only the field probe has `trap restore_snapshot EXIT`, not artifact deletion), lack `leaked-artifacts.log`, and lack partial-setup tracking, but the plan does not state this gap as the justification for a new harness over extension. Without this rationale the plan may be reinventing prior art.
findings.location: Harness (guaranteed bilateral teardown) section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the setup-probe assertion to match the actual `delete_issue` return shape: check `result.get("status") == "deleted"` (not exact dict equality), and note that a 404 returns `{"status": "not_found"}` (not an exception) while only 403 raises `PermissionError`.
findings.finding: The setup-probe assertion 'assert `client.delete_issue` returns `{"status":"deleted"}`' is an unverified capability assertion. The actual `acli.py` implementation (lines 710-718) returns `{"status": "deleted", "key": jira_key}` on success (extra `"key"` field not mentioned) and returns `{"status": "not_found", "key": jira_key}` — not raising — for a 404/already-deleted case. Only a 403 raises `PermissionError`. The plan's probe assertion as written would fail if the harness does an exact dict equality check, and the plan implies 404 is a loud PermissionError when it is not.
findings.location: Harness section — Setup probe: 'assert `client.delete_issue` returns `{"status":"deleted"}`; NOTE `acli.py:721-723` RAISES PermissionError on missing delete perm'
findings.suggested_fix: Assert `client.delete_issue(key).get('status') == 'deleted'`; add a note that `not_found` is idempotent success and only `PermissionError` (403) indicates a scope misconfiguration.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the teardown-retry capability assertion: either confirm that `_call_with_retry` can wrap `delete_issue` after catching `CalledProcessError`/`PermissionError` at the call site, or document that a bespoke 3x loop is necessary because the app-layer retry helpers are typed to `JiraAPIError`/`HTTPError` and do not cover the subprocess error domain.
findings.finding: The Round-6 correction recommends reusing `_call_with_retry` from `dispatch_one`/`batch_dispatch` for the teardown 3x exponential-backoff retry, but this is an unverified capability assertion. `_call_with_retry` (dispatch_one.py:104-173) wraps a callable that raises `JiraAPIError` or `urllib.error.HTTPError` with typed status codes — it is designed for REST/ACLI app-layer calls that raise those typed exceptions. `client.delete_issue` (acli.py) raises `PermissionError` on 403 and re-raises `CalledProcessError` on other non-zero exits; it does NOT raise `JiraAPIError`. Wrapping `delete_issue` in `_call_with_retry` would thus never retry on `CalledProcessError` (the actual transient failure mode), making the reuse recommendation incorrect for the teardown context.
findings.location: Round-6 corrections — 'TEARDOWN RETRY: reuse an existing backoff helper (e.g. the app-layer retry in `dispatch_one`/`batch_dispatch`) rather than a bespoke 3x loop'
findings.suggested_fix: Use a thin bespoke retry wrapper for teardown that catches `(CalledProcessError, PermissionError, Exception)` with exponential backoff, or add a `delete_issue_with_retry` adapter that catches subprocess errors and re-raises as a retryable form before passing to `_call_with_retry`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `acli auth login` pre-step to the harness setup (mirroring `reconcile-bridge.yml:182-185`: `echo "$JIRA_API_TOKEN" | acli jira auth login --site "$JIRA_URL" --email "$JIRA_USER" --token`) and document it in the Isolation/credentials section alongside the secret-injection note.
findings.finding: The plan's 'Isolation, credentials' section says the token is 'injected via secret, never logged/committed', but does not document the required `acli jira auth login` pre-step. The ACLI binary authenticates by reading a config file written by `acli auth login` (confirmed: reconcile-bridge.yml:182-185 runs `echo "$JIRA_API_TOKEN" | acli jira auth login --site "$JIRA_URL" --email "$JIRA_USER" --token`). Without this step, every `_run_acli` subprocess call will fail with an auth error (exit 401), making the entire harness inoperable. The AcliClient docstring (acli.py:224) confirms 'Credentials are injected into the subprocess environment on each call so ACLI can authenticate without requiring prior acli auth setup' — but this refers to env vars forwarded by `_build_env`, which only copies `os.environ`; the binary still requires its config-file login for the ACLI CLI path used by `delete_issue`, `create_issue`, etc.
findings.location: Harness / Isolation, credentials section; acli.py:224,250-252; reconcile-bridge.yml:182-185
findings.suggested_fix: In the Harness section, add a Setup step before the capability probe: `echo "$JIRA_API_TOKEN" | acli jira auth login --site "$JIRA_URL" --email "$JIRA_USER" --token`. This mirrors the existing CI workflow step and is the required precondition for all ACLI subprocess calls. Note it in the credentials section alongside the secret-injection guidance.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare (in the harness design note / ADR) that all CI artifact outputs (JUnit report, leaked-artifacts.log, stdout/stderr logs) are either masked by the CI runner for `JIRA_API_TOKEN` or scrubbed by the harness before writing — and add a harness step that asserts the token value does not appear in any written artifact.
findings.finding: The plan does not declare that harness CI artifacts (stdout/stderr logs, the JSON/JUnit report, and leaked-artifacts.log) are masked or scrubbed for the JIRA_API_TOKEN value. If acli or Python subprocesses include credential material in error tracebacks or verbose output captured by the harness, those could surface in unmasked CI artifact files — a residual secret-lifecycle gap on the boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: Harness section / Teardown-failure fallback / AC line: 'writes a JSON/JUnit report to a named path attached to the ticket'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a post-run sanitisation step in the harness (bash: `grep -rF "${JIRA_API_TOKEN}" artifacts/ && { echo 'TOKEN LEAKED IN ARTIFACT'; exit 1; } || true`) and register `JIRA_API_TOKEN` as a masked secret in the CI runner config so it is automatically redacted from log output.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the blast-radius assertion: remove `census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` from AC line 2 and replace with `check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings=N, max_acting_fraction=0.09).allowed == False` (per Round-6), or call `census(max_acting_fraction=0.09, total_bindings=N)['breaker']['allowed'] == False` with explicit kwargs.
findings.finding: AC line 2 retains the unguarded `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` form that Round-6 corrections explicitly warn will KeyError when the breaker is not evaluated on an acting pass; the two instructions in the same plan directly contradict each other, leaving the executor without a canonical, executable assertion.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria line 2 (blast-radius assertion) vs. Round-6 corrections section
findings.suggested_fix: In AC line 2, replace `classify.census()['breaker']['allowed']==False` with `classify.check_blast_radius(decisions, total_bindings=N, max_acting_fraction=0.09).allowed == False` where `decisions` is the list from the mass-change scenario walk.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the C4 injection mechanism explicitly: state whether the live-harness C4 assertion uses a dedicated subprocess-script stub (like `_fake_cmd` in unit tests), a monkeypatch of `acli_subprocess.time.sleep` only, or a separate sub-test that runs in isolation from the live-Jira phases.
findings.finding: The C4 signal definition instructs the executor to 'monkey-patch the LIVE retry path' and assert 'a simulated 429 exit -> backoff -> success' inside what is otherwise a live-Jira harness, but no injection protocol is specified: the plan does not say whether to monkeypatch `acli_subprocess.time.sleep`, mock the subprocess Popen call, or use the `_fake_cmd` subprocess-script trick from the unit tests — all three are fundamentally incompatible with a fully-live ACLI execution path.
findings.location: Signal definitions section, C4 paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete ProvenanceLedger seeding protocol to the echo-suppression signal definition: specify which (key, side, value) entries the harness must record after the first pass (e.g., field names written outbound, with side='local' or 'remote') so the second-pass mutation_count==0 assertion is non-vacuous.
findings.finding: The echo-suppression signal says 'field/status writes are baseline-seeded via the ProvenanceLedger' and 'harness pre-seeds the baseline as a real pass would', but the plan never specifies what (key, side, value) triples the harness must seed into the ProvenanceLedger to constitute a valid baseline — without this, the executor cannot write a correct seeding step.
findings.location: Signal definitions section, 'Echo suppression' paragraph

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit fallback protocol for the C1 signal: specify whether the harness should (a) skip C1 with a logged warning if feline-wandering-dassierat is pre-manifest, (b) fail loudly requiring a re-run after C1 lands, or (c) probe C1's ticket status at runtime and conditionally execute.
findings.finding: The C1 signal instructs the executor to 'assert against the exact conflict record C1 (feline-wandering-dassierat) lands' and 'reference C1's implemented signal at validation time', but no fallback protocol is specified for the case where C1 has not yet landed when the harness runs — the plan says 'C1 is pre-manifest; do NOT hard-code an unverified field' without saying what the harness should do instead (skip C1, fail, poll for C1's manifest?).
findings.location: Signal definitions section, C1 paragraph

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
